key_findings: THE MOAT IS AGGREGATED CONSUMPTION MOMENTS, NOT RATINGS. The two proven comps monetize the exact same asset: Untappd Insights sells B2B analytics off '10M+ registered users' and 'over 1 billion all-time consumption moments,' explicitly branded 'aggregated, anonymized, and privacy compliant.' Vivino (71M users, 70M+ reviews, ~$600-800M valuation per Bloomberg) sells 'aggregated, anonymized insights to partners like supermarkets and wineries' as a distinct revenue line. The sellable asset is not any single user's list of liked beers - it is the DENSITY of geo/time-stamped consumption events per market that lets a brewery/distributor see product velocity, style migration, and demand pockets. Your day-one schema must be event-centric (one row per check-in with beer_id, style, ABV/IBU, geo, timestamp, occasion tag, flavor tags, rating), not profile-centric.

COLLECT FROM DAY ONE - THE TASTE-GRAPH SCHEMA. Per check-in event: beer/SKU (mapped to a canonical brand/brewery/style taxonomy - this taxonomy is itself an asset), style, ABV, IBU, rating (0-5), free-text + structured flavor tags (hoppy/malty/sour/fruity/roasty), geo (lat/long or venue_id + on-premise vs off-premise flag), precise timestamp (day-of-week/daypart/season are high-value to CPG), occasion/context (home, bar, restaurant, event, sports), and purchase-intent signals (saved, wishlisted, 'where to buy' clicks, price paid if captured). Per user: derived preference vectors (top styles, ABV/IBU comfort band, novelty-seeking vs loyalist, price tier). The compounding effect: each new check-in improves per-market density AND the recommendation flywheel (Vivino's documented 'more users -> more data -> better AI recs -> more engagement -> more data' loop), so the moat is (a) hard-to-replicate longitudinal per-market density and (b) a proprietary SKU taxonomy competitors would have to rebuild.

WHO BUYS AND WHY - THREE DISTINCT ACQUIRER CLASSES, ALL REAL. (1) Vertical roll-ups / strategic operators: Next Glass acquired Untappd (2016) and then bought BeerAdvocate's assets (2020) to consolidate the beer data category, later taking a growth investment from Providence Strategic Growth - the thesis is owning the category's demand-side dataset + the B2B analytics product (Untappd for Business, ~20,000 venues, $899-$1,199/yr). (2) Big Beer / CPG for first-party data: AB InBev has built a CDP holding ~100M first-party customer records and $52.5B GMV through BEES (2025) - brewers are actively buying/building consumer-data capability because craft-vs-macro competition is now data-driven (per Bold Business, brewery analytics is 'Big Beer's secret weapon'). (3) Loyalty/payments/martech platforms: Mastercard acquired SessionM (2019, raised ~$100M) purely to bolt a consumer-engagement/loyalty data layer onto its network. Precedent even in the long tail: Proof Network Ventures acquired Drync (2019) explicitly citing 'hoards of important data' that 'retailers, wholesalers and brands can make use of.' A market-research buyer (Circana/NIQ) is also plausible: they already sell alcohol POS + consumer-panel data (Circana Unify+, NABCA depletions) and would pay for a complementary attitudinal/occasion layer they cannot get from scanner data.

VALUATION LEVERS ARE DATA DENSITY x EXCLUSIVITY x CONTRACTED B2B REVENUE - NOT DAU ALONE. Raw DAU matters, but acquirers pay premiums for: (a) per-market data density (a brewery/distributor cares about 'do you have enough check-ins in MY territory to be statistically useful' - so geographic depth beats thin national breadth); (b) exclusivity/proprietariness of the SKU taxonomy and the panel; (c) recurring contracted B2B revenue - the M&A literature is explicit that 'high proportion of contracted recurring revenue' and 'geographic exclusivity/market position' are the most consistent multiple-expansion factors because they de-risk post-close revenue. Untappd's model shows the playbook: convert the consumer app's exhaust into a subscription B2B analytics product (Untappd Insights + Untappd for Business) so the acquirer buys a data ASSET plus a revenue STREAM. Data also DECAYS (contact/behavioral data decays ~22-28%/yr), so demonstrable refresh velocity (live check-in flow) is a valuation input - a stale one-time dump is worth far less than a live sensor network.

HARD GUARDRAIL - YOU CAN ONLY SELL WHAT YOU COLLECTED WITH TRANSFERABLE CONSENT, AND 2025 LAW TIGHTENED THIS. California AB 1824 (effective Jan 1, 2025) now REQUIRES an acquirer to honor every opt-out of 'sale'/'sharing' that California residents made to the seller BEFORE closing - so opt-outs travel with the data and the buyer inherits them; you must therefore maintain an auditable, exportable log of every opt-out from day one or you materially impair (or block) a future sale. GDPR is stricter still: 'change of control' transfer needs a lawful basis and the privacy notice must have disclosed that data may be transferred in a merger/acquisition; a blanket 'we will never sell your data' promise in your policy can legally PROHIBIT the asset sale. CCPA/CPRA does not ban transfer-on-M&A but may require notice + opt-out. Bottom line: the consent architecture is a gating asset - get it wrong and the data becomes legally unsellable regardless of how dense it is.

CONSENT ARCHITECTURE TO BUILD DAY ONE (enables a future data sale or company acquisition): (1) Privacy Policy 'Business Transfer' clause explicitly stating personal data may be transferred/disclosed as a business asset in a merger, acquisition, financing, reorganization, bankruptcy, or sale of all/part of assets, and that the successor may continue processing under this policy. (2) NEVER include an absolute 'we will never sell or transfer your data' line - it forecloses the exit. (3) Terms of Service ASSIGNMENT clause: 'We may assign this agreement and our rights/obligations to a successor or acquirer without your consent.' (4) A durable, timestamped consent + opt-out ledger (who consented to what processing/sharing, and every do-not-sell/do-not-share request) that is exportable to a buyer - required to satisfy AB 1824 and to survive diligence. (5) Purpose language broad enough to cover 'aggregated/anonymized analytics sold to third parties' and clean-room use, so B2B monetization is pre-consented. (6) A robust de-identification/aggregation pipeline: selling AGGREGATED, ANONYMIZED insights (the Untappd/Vivino model) sidesteps most 'sale of personal information' friction and is the safest, highest-margin monetization path - only the aggregate layer, never raw PII, goes to CPG buyers via reports/dashboards or a data clean room.
recommendations: Design the database EVENT-FIRST, not profile-first. One immutable row per check-in: {user_id, beer_id(canonical), brewery_id, style, abv, ibu, rating, flavor_tags[], geo, venue_id, on_off_premise, occasion, price_paid, purchase_intent_flags, timestamp}. This is the literal shape of the sellable asset (Untappd sells 'consumption moments,' not profiles). Build and OWN a canonical beer/brewery/style taxonomy from day one - the SKU-mapping layer is a moat competitors must rebuild and is what makes your data joinable to CPG systems.

Instrument the high-value CPG signals most apps skip: daypart/day-of-week/season, on-premise vs off-premise, occasion/context, and purchase-intent clicks ('where to buy,' price paid). Scanner-data buyers (Circana/NIQ) already have POS; what they'll PAY you for is the attitudinal + occasion + pre-purchase-intent layer they cannot derive from till data.

Optimize for per-market DENSITY, not vanity national DAU. Pick 2-4 launch metros and saturate them so you can credibly tell a brewery/distributor 'we have statistically meaningful check-in volume in your territory.' A dense single-market panel is more acquirable than thin national breadth because it de-risks the buyer's use case.

Stand up the B2B product early, even in beta - an 'Insights'-style reports/dashboard layer (mirror Untappd Insights + Untappd for Business). Convert data exhaust into CONTRACTED recurring B2B revenue (breweries, distributors, retailers). Recurring contracts are the single biggest documented multiple-expansion lever in an eventual sale; you want to sell an asset AND a revenue stream, not just a dataset.

Ship the transfer-enabling legal stack at launch, drafted by counsel: (a) Privacy Policy business-transfer clause covering merger/acquisition/asset-sale; (b) ToS assignment clause allowing assignment to a successor without user consent; (c) purpose language covering aggregated/anonymized analytics sold to third parties and clean-room collaboration; (d) explicitly avoid any absolute 'never sell/transfer' promise. Have counsel confirm the policy does not contradict the intended data monetization - a contradiction can void the exit.

Build a durable consent + opt-out LEDGER from record one (timestamped, exportable, per-purpose, including every CCPA do-not-sell/do-not-share request). California AB 1824 (in force since Jan 1, 2025) makes these opt-outs bind any future acquirer, and a clean, exportable ledger is a diligence pass/fail item that directly affects price.

Make the DEFAULT commercial product the aggregated, anonymized layer delivered via reports/dashboards or a data clean room - never raw PII. This is exactly how Untappd and Vivino monetize, it minimizes 'sale of personal information' exposure under CCPA/GDPR, carries the highest margin, and is the format acquirers and CPG buyers actually want.

Run the app through a light M&A-readiness lens quarterly: track (1) check-ins/day and 90-day retention, (2) per-market density in target metros, (3) B2B ARR and logo count, (4) refresh velocity (data is decaying at ~22-28%/yr, so live flow is the asset), and (5) consent-ledger completeness. These five are the numbers a Next-Glass- or Circana-type buyer will underwrite.
risks: A 'free beer app' collects sensitive-adjacent data (alcohol consumption, precise geolocation, age). This raises age-gating/COPPA-style and sensitive-data obligations; mishandled, it can trigger regulatory action AND make the data toxic/unsellable. Mitigate with hard age verification and geolocation-consent granularity from day one.

Legal foreclosure risk: if the launch privacy policy contains an absolute 'we will never sell/transfer your data' or lacks a business-transfer + ToS-assignment clause, the primary asset may be legally un-transferable in an acquisition - the entire exit thesis collapses. This is a drafting error that is very hard to fix retroactively because you cannot re-consent a churned user base.

AB 1824 (Jan 1 2025) inheritance risk: a buyer inherits all pre-close opt-outs and faces $2,500 (negligent) / $7,500 (intentional) per-violation penalties. If your opt-out logging is incomplete or not exportable, a buyer will discount heavily or walk in diligence.

GDPR exposure if you take EU users: change-of-control transfer needs a disclosed lawful basis; cross-border transfer of EU personal data to a US acquirer is restricted. If EU users are a material share of the panel, the transferable dataset may be smaller than headline DAU implies. Consider region-scoping the consent architecture.

Data-density trap: buyers underwrite USABLE density per market, not raw registered users. A large but shallow/inactive base (low check-ins/user, high churn) is worth far less than the headline suggests - and behavioral data decays 22-28%/yr, so an inactive base decays into near-worthlessness.

Competitive/incumbency risk: Untappd/Next Glass already owns the beer-check-in category with 10M+ users and 1B+ moments, and BeerBoard/Circana/NIQ own the supply-side and POS data. A new entrant's data is only acquirable if it captures a signal the incumbents lack (e.g., richer occasion/intent tagging, an under-served geography, or a demographic the incumbents miss).

Anonymization is not a free pass: re-identification of geo+time+consumption traces is a known risk; regulators increasingly treat granular 'anonymized' behavioral data as personal data. Weak de-identification can reclassify your 'anonymous' product as a regulated 'sale of personal information,' undermining both compliance and margin.
data.details:
STRATEGIC FRAME: The proven, real-world exits confirm the founder's thesis but sharpen it. The sellable asset is not a pile of user favorites - it is a LIVE, GEO/TIME-STAMPED CONSUMPTION-EVENT PANEL plus a proprietary SKU taxonomy plus (ideally) a contracted B2B analytics revenue stream. Untappd Insights literally packages '10M+ users / 1B+ consumption moments' as 'aggregated, anonymized, privacy compliant' B2B analytics; Vivino runs the identical model in wine (71M users, 70M reviews, ~$600-800M valuation) and sells aggregated insights to supermarkets/wineries as a discrete revenue line. Build to that shape from record one.

COMPS / WHO BUYS AND WHY (all real): (1) Category roll-up: Next Glass bought Untappd (2016) + BeerAdvocate assets (2020), monetizes via Untappd for Business (~20k venues, $899-$1,199/yr) and Untappd Insights, then took a Providence Strategic Growth investment - thesis = own the demand-side beer dataset + B2B product. (2) Big Beer first-party data: AB InBev's BEES did $52.5B GMV in 2025 with a CDP holding ~100M customer records; brewery analytics is now competitive weaponry, so a macro brewer buying a consumer taste-graph is a live rationale. (3) Loyalty/payments/martech: Mastercard bought SessionM (2019, ~$100M raised) to add consumer-engagement data. (4) Long-tail precedent: Proof Network Ventures bought Drync (2019) explicitly for its data 'hoards' useful to 'retailers, wholesalers and brands.' (5) Market-research buyer: Circana (Unify+, NABCA depletions) / NIQ already sell alcohol POS + panel data and would value a complementary attitudinal/occasion/intent layer scanner data can't produce.

VALUATION LEVERS (ranked): per-market data density > exclusivity/proprietary taxonomy > contracted recurring B2B revenue > refresh velocity > raw DAU. M&A guidance is explicit that contracted recurring revenue and geographic exclusivity/market position are the most consistent multiple-expansion factors; behavioral data decays ~22-28%/yr, so a LIVE flow (not a static dump) is what holds value.

CONSENT ARCHITECTURE (the gating asset - build day one): (a) Privacy Policy business-transfer clause covering merger/acquisition/asset-sale/bankruptcy with successor-processing language; (b) ToS assignment clause permitting assignment to an acquirer without user consent; (c) purpose language pre-authorizing aggregated/anonymized third-party analytics + clean-room use; (d) NO absolute 'never sell/transfer' promise (it can legally block the sale); (e) a durable, timestamped, EXPORTABLE consent + opt-out ledger. The 2025 legal reality: California AB 1824 (in force Jan 1 2025) forces any acquirer to honor pre-close CCPA opt-outs (penalties $2,500-$7,500/violation), so opt-out logging is a diligence pass/fail; GDPR requires a disclosed lawful basis for change-of-control transfer and restricts EU->US transfer; CCPA/CPRA permits M&A transfer but may require notice/opt-out. Safest, highest-margin monetization = sell only the AGGREGATED, ANONYMIZED layer via reports/dashboards/clean rooms, never raw PII - which also minimizes 'sale of personal information' exposure.

NET: The data-asset thesis is sound and there are real acquirers and real comps. The two things that will actually determine whether it becomes a sale are (1) per-market density + a B2B revenue stream wrapped around the event panel, and (2) a transfer-enabling legal/consent stack shipped at launch. Get the legal stack wrong and even a dense, valuable dataset becomes legally unsellable.

key_findings: Market is already proven, not hypothetical. Untappd Insights (owned by Next Glass) sells this exact 'territory' asset today: style/flavor trend monitoring, social sentiment analysis, and location-based consumption data, marketed as 'aggregated, anonymized, and privacy compliant' to breweries, beer brands, and distributors, drawing on 10M+ registered users and 1B+ all-time 'consumption moments.' In 2025 users checked into 1.5M unique beverages across 2.6M locations. This validates both the consumer feature and the B2B product and sets the competitive bar (source: insights.untappd.com, nextglass.co).

The B2B demand-signal thesis is real and timely. 2026 industry data shows sharp, sellable directional shifts: hazy IPA palate fatigue with a West Coast IPA revival, growth in 'Session Hazy' / Hazy Pale (4-5.5% ABV), broad lager resurgence, and explosive N/A beer growth (~33.7% YoY on-premise). U.S. craft production fell ~4% in 2025 to ~22.0M barrels even as craft's volume share ticked up to 13.4%, and hyperlocal small breweries are the growth pocket. These are exactly the 'hazy IPA demand up 30% in Austin' style signals breweries/distributors/brands will pay for (source: Brewers Association, Kalsec, GetBackbar, Beer Connoisseur).

There is a clean, proven NLP structure for turning free-text reviews into flavor/sentiment intelligence. The canonical BeerAdvocate corpus (~1.5M reviews) is already organized around four aspects (appearance, aroma, palate, taste) plus overall, which is the natural schema for aspect-based sentiment analysis (ABSA). Modern practice pairs a fine-tuned encoder (BERT-class) for aspect+polarity extraction with an LLM pass for nuanced/structured cases; 2024 flavor research further groups descriptors by origin (malt, hops, yeast, wild flora). This gives a defensible flavor-lexicon + ABSA pipeline rather than crude star-average sentiment (source: BeerAdvocate dataset literature, PMC beer-flavor ML study, 2024 ABSA papers).

Privacy is a solved engineering problem with documented thresholds, and it is a feature to sell, not just a constraint. k-anonymity with k>=10 is the accepted floor (healthcare pseudonymization at k=10 cut max re-identification risk from 100% to ~1.09%; location-privacy studies use a 10-household minimum). Aggregate outputs should add calibrated noise (differential privacy) exactly as data clean rooms do in 2025 CPG pilots, and cross-party enrichment (e.g., matching to distributor depletion data) should run inside a clean room so no raw PII is exposed. This lets the product answer 'what's hot in Austin' without ever exposing that a specific user drinks a specific beer at a specific bar (source: Springer k-anonymity study, ScienceDirect, eMarketer/Duality clean-room coverage).

The individual-level raw data (who / where / when / exact venue) is the liability; the geographic aggregate is the asset. The design must enforce a hard boundary: personal timelines, friend feeds, and precise venue check-ins stay in the consumer app under the user's control; only k-anonymized, minimum-sample, noised roll-ups by geo x style x time cross into the sellable layer. Getting this boundary right is simultaneously the privacy safeguard, the legal posture (GDPR/CCPA 'aggregate/de-identified' carve-outs), and the trust story that keeps consumers checking in.
recommendations: Build the consumer feature as a geo-native discovery layer, not a social feed add-on. Ship: (1) Local Leaderboards - top-rated + most-checked-in beers/breweries filtered to the user's city/neighborhood with an adjustable radius; (2) 'What's Hot Near You' - a momentum feed ranking beers by check-in velocity and rating-trend over the last 7/30 days within the user's area, not all-time volume (all-time just resurfaces Tree House/Other Half everywhere); (3) Regional Taste Maps - a choropleth/heatmap where color encodes the dominant style, average rating, or a specific style's share of local check-ins, with a style toggle ('show me where hazy IPA over-indexes'); (4) Trending Styles by City - a ranked, week-over-week style-share board per metro; (5) Brewery Popularity by Area - local reputation independent of national scale so a strong regional brewery outranks a distant giant. Make the map the home surface - it is the differentiator and the demo that sells the B2B product.

Package the B2B/sellable product in three ascending tiers keyed off the SAME aggregation engine that powers the consumer maps (build once, sell the roll-ups). Tier 1 - Regional Trend Reports (self-serve dashboard + scheduled exports): style-share, demand momentum, and sentiment by DMA/metro/state over rolling windows, with the headline being ready-made signals like 'West Coast IPA check-in share +X% QoQ in Denver' or 'N/A category +Y% in the Southeast.' Tier 2 - Competitive & Distribution Intelligence: a brewery/brand sees where its beers over- or under-index vs. category and vs. named competitors by region, plus 'white space' maps (high demand for a style, low local supply) to guide taproom siting and distribution expansion. Tier 3 - Custom / Clean-Room: bespoke pulls and privacy-safe matching of the check-in demand layer against a distributor's depletion or a brand's shipment data inside a data clean room, for closed-loop 'does regional buzz predict sell-through' analytics. Price ascending SaaS seats for T1-2 and per-engagement for T3; buyers are breweries, distributors, beverage brands, and their agencies.

Ship the demand-signal metrics as named, defensible indices so buyers can trust and cite them. Define: Style Demand Index (share of regional check-ins for a style, indexed 100 = national average, so >100 = over-indexes locally); Momentum (period-over-period change in that share, the 'up 30%' number); Flavor Sentiment Score (ABSA-derived net positivity per flavor attribute, e.g., 'juicy/tropical' vs 'harsh/astringent', per region); Local Favorite Rank (rating x local velocity, PII-safe). Always publish the underlying sample size and window with each figure and suppress any cell below the minimum-sample threshold - buyers in beverage are wary of thin-data claims, so transparent n is a selling point.

Implement the flavor/sentiment NLP as a two-stage pipeline with a curated beer flavor lexicon. Stage 1: a fine-tuned encoder model does aspect-based sentiment analysis over review text, tagging spans to the appearance/aroma/palate/taste/overall aspects and to a beer-specific descriptor ontology (malt, hop, yeast, ferment/wild, off-flavor groupings from 2024 flavor research). Stage 2: an LLM pass handles nuance, negation, and normalizes slang ('crushable', 'dank', 'catty') into the ontology, and flags low-confidence cases for review. Aggregate the tagged sentiment ONLY at the geo x style x window level with the same k-threshold as ratings. Run Stage 1 on-device where feasible (extract structured flavor tags locally, upload only the anonymized tags, not raw text) to strengthen the privacy story and cut server text-processing cost; keep the heavier LLM pass server-side on already-de-identified text.

Enforce a strict two-plane data architecture with a hard privacy boundary between them. Personal plane (individual, consented, user-controlled): raw check-ins, exact venue, timestamp, friends, personal history - never sold, never leaves per-user scope; do coarse flavor-tag extraction on-device. Aggregate plane (sellable): server-side roll-ups keyed by geo bucket x style x rolling window, gated by three rules applied at query time - (a) k-anonymity with k>=10 minimum distinct users per cell, (b) minimum-sample suppression that drops or up-levels any geo/time cell below threshold (a rural county rolls up to region; a slow week rolls up to month), and (c) calibrated differential-privacy noise on published counts/shares. Geo buckets should snap to standard units (metro/DMA/ZIP3/H3 hex) - never raw lat/long - so 'near you' works for consumers while the sold layer can never re-identify a person, a single venue's exact patrons, or one user's habits.
risks: Re-identification via sparse cells is the核心 risk: a rarely-checked beer in a small town, or a single dominant venue in a low-density area, can de facto identify one person even inside an 'aggregate.' Mitigation: enforce k>=10 distinct users AND a minimum venue count per cell, auto-roll-up sparse geos/windows, and add DP noise - never publish a cell just because it technically passed a rating count.

Selling location + consumption data invites regulatory and reputational exposure (GDPR/CCPA, plus alcohol-consumption being arguably sensitive-adjacent). If consumers feel their drinking is being sold, check-in volume - the entire asset - collapses. Mitigation: explicit consent and a clear consumer-facing 'your check-ins power local trends, never sold individually' promise, honor opt-out from the aggregate layer, keep the personal plane sacrosanct, and lean on the de-identified/aggregate legal carve-outs by actually meeting their technical bar.

Sample-bias and representativeness: check-in users skew toward enthusiasts and hazy/hype styles, so 'regional demand' from app data is demand among loggers, not the whole market - a brewery could over-trust a signal that reflects the app's crowd. Mitigation: disclose that signals are check-in-based, calibrate against external anchors (Brewers Association production, distributor depletion in clean-room engagements) where possible, and frame outputs as directional momentum/sentiment rather than absolute market share.

Incumbent lock-in: Untappd Insights / Next Glass already owns the data-network-effect position with 1B+ consumption moments and existing brewery relationships. A new entrant with thin check-in volume will produce noisy, easily-suppressed regional cells and weak signals. Mitigation: win a specific territory or niche densely first (hyperlocal depth beats thin national breadth), or acquire scale via partnerships, before promising nationwide demand intelligence.

NLP flavor/sentiment errors at scale can produce confidently wrong 'insights' - sarcasm, negation ('not too bitter'), and evolving slang can flip polarity, and a mislabeled off-flavor could unfairly ding a brewery in a sold report. Mitigation: keep humans in the loop on the descriptor ontology, hold out a labeled validation set, publish confidence/coverage alongside sentiment scores, and never surface a per-brewery negative claim below the sample threshold.
data.details:
TERRITORY DIMENSION - full design.

WHY THIS IS REAL: Untappd Insights (Next Glass) already sells this exact asset - "style and flavor trend monitoring, social sentiment analysis, location-based consumption information," marketed as "aggregated, anonymized, and privacy compliant," to breweries/brands/distributors off 10M+ users and 1B+ consumption moments. So the design below is a competitive product spec, not a green-field bet.

(1) CONSUMER FEATURE - GEO-NATIVE DISCOVERY. Home surface is a map, not a feed. Five pillars: Local Leaderboards (top-rated + most-checked beers/breweries within an adjustable radius of the user); "What's Hot Near You" (ranks by 7/30-day check-in VELOCITY and rating-trend, not all-time volume - velocity surfaces genuinely rising local beers instead of re-showing the same national hype breweries everywhere); Regional Taste Maps/Heatmaps (choropleth over metro/DMA/ZIP3/H3-hex buckets; color encodes dominant style, avg rating, or a chosen style's local share; toggle "show where hazy IPA over-indexes"); Trending Styles by City (week-over-week style-share ranking per metro); Brewery Popularity by Area (local reputation decoupled from national scale so a great regional brewery outranks a distant giant). The consumer map is also the live demo that sells the B2B product.

(2) B2B / SELLABLE PRODUCT - same aggregation engine, three tiers. T1 Regional Trend Reports: style-share, demand momentum, sentiment by DMA/metro/state on rolling windows, headline signals like "West Coast IPA check-in share +X% QoQ in Denver," "N/A +33.7% YoY Southeast." T2 Competitive & Distribution Intelligence: where a brand over/under-indexes vs category and vs named competitors by region; "white space" maps (high demand, low local supply) to guide taproom siting + distribution. T3 Custom / Clean-Room: bespoke pulls + privacy-safe matching of the demand layer against distributor depletion / brand shipment data inside a data clean room for closed-loop "does buzz predict sell-through." Named metrics buyers can cite: Style Demand Index (local share indexed to national=100), Momentum (period-over-period delta - the "up 30%" figure), Flavor Sentiment Score (ABSA net positivity per attribute per region), Local Favorite Rank. Every figure ships with sample size n and window; sub-threshold cells suppressed. 2026 context makes these salable NOW: hazy fatigue + West Coast revival, Session Hazy/Hazy Pale growth, lager resurgence, N/A boom, craft production -4% to ~22.0M bbl but share up to 13.4%, hyperlocal breweries growing.

(3) AGGREGATION WITHOUT EXPOSING INDIVIDUALS - two-plane architecture with a hard boundary. PERSONAL PLANE (never sold): raw check-ins, exact venue, timestamp, friends, personal history; user-controlled; do coarse flavor-tag NLP ON-DEVICE and upload only anonymized structured tags, not raw text. AGGREGATE PLANE (sellable): server roll-ups keyed geo-bucket x style x rolling-window, gated at QUERY TIME by three rules - (a) k-anonymity k>=10 distinct users per cell (k=10 is the documented floor: cut healthcare re-id risk 100%->1.09%; location studies use 10-household min) PLUS a minimum distinct-venue count so one dominant bar can't be de-anonymized; (b) minimum-sample suppression that auto-rolls-up sparse geos/windows (rural county -> region, slow week -> month); (c) calibrated differential-privacy noise on published counts/shares, exactly as 2025 CPG data-clean-rooms do. Geo always snaps to standard units (metro/DMA/ZIP3/H3 hex), never raw lat/long.

NLP ON REVIEW TEXT: two-stage aspect-based sentiment analysis on the BeerAdvocate-style 4-aspect schema (appearance/aroma/palate/taste + overall). Stage 1 fine-tuned encoder (BERT-class) tags spans to aspects + a beer flavor ontology (malt/hop/yeast/ferment-wild/off-flavor groups from 2024 flavor research), runnable on-device. Stage 2 server-side LLM pass handles negation, sarcasm, and slang normalization ("crushable/dank/catty" -> ontology), flags low-confidence for human review. Sentiment aggregated only at geo x style x window under the same k-threshold. On-device Stage 1 doubles as privacy hardening (raw text need never leave the phone) and server cost reduction.

RELEVANT FILE PATHS: none - this is a net-new product design; no existing repo was read for this task.

https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10207-024-00838-8 - k-anonymity study: k=10 pseudonymization reduced max re-identification risk from 100% to ~1.09%
https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/k-anonymity - k-anonymity definition, minimum group size / equivalence-class threshold, location-based-services application
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10966102/ - ML on beer flavor: descriptors grouped by origin (malt/hops/yeast/wild) and sensory aspect

https://www.emarketer.com/content/faq-on-data-clean-rooms-how-retail-media-driving-adoption-marketers-demand-proof - data clean rooms in 2025 CPG measurement, privacy-safe first/second-party matching without exposing raw PII
https://dualitytech.com/blog/data-clean-room/ - data clean rooms + differential privacy (calibrated noise on aggregates) for privacy-safe collaboration

key_findings: POI CATEGORIES ARE FIRST-CLASS FOR US. MKPointOfInterestCategory (a String-backed type) ships exactly the categories a beer app needs: .brewery, .bar, .nightlife, plus adjacent .winery, .distillery, .cafe, .restaurant, .foodMarket. You compose an MKPointOfInterestFilter(including: [.brewery, .bar, .nightlife, .winery, .distillery]) and either (a) apply it to the Map as pointOfInterestFilter so Apple's own basemap POIs are limited to beer venues, or (b) feed it to a search to fetch results. This gives us a free, globally-maintained brewery dataset from Apple with zero backend.

TWO SEARCH PATHS, PICK BY INTENT. MKLocalPointsOfInterestRequest(center:radius:) + pointOfInterestFilter is the correct primitive for 'breweries near me' — it is purpose-built for category search with NO natural-language query and returns MKMapItems in a radius (max radius ~50 km / 25000 m per request). MKLocalSearch.Request with naturalLanguageQuery='brewery' + a .region is the fallback for text search / the search bar. Both run through MKLocalSearch().start() and return [MKMapItem]. Results are rate-limited by Apple, so cache and debounce on region-change (onMapCameraChange).

SWiftUI MAP IS PRODUCTION-READY BUT HAS THREE HARD GAPS vs UIKit MKMapView. The modern SwiftUI Map (iOS 17+) gives declarative Marker (system balloon w/ systemImage+tint), Annotation (arbitrary SwiftUI view), MapPolygon/MapCircle overlays, MapCameraPosition, onMapCameraChange, selection binding, and .mapStyle. BUT SwiftUI Map still has NO built-in annotation clustering, NO custom cluster views, and NO MKOverlayRenderer (so no true GPU heatmap). All three require dropping to UIKit MKMapView via UIViewRepresentable. This is the single most important architectural fact for this feature.

NATIVE PLACE CARD IS FREE (iOS 18+). The mapItemDetailSelectionAccessory / MapItemDetailSelectionAccessory API (WWDC24 'Unlock the power of places') renders Apple's rich Place Card — photos, hours, phone, ratings, directions button — as a .callout or .sheet directly from a selected MKMapItem, in SwiftUI/UIKit/AppKit, even without a Map. We layer OUR beer/tap data on top: use the Place Card for hours/photos/directions, and render our own SwiftUI callout section for current taplist, check-in count, and 'X people drinking here now'.

LOOK AROUND IS TURNKEY AND ON-BRAND. MKLookAroundSceneRequest(mapItem:) (or a coordinate) -> await .scene, then LookAroundPreview(scene:) as a thumbnail and .lookAroundViewer(isPresented:scene:) for full screen. Coverage is city-limited, so the scene is often nil — the spec must gate the Look Around thumbnail on a non-nil scene and degrade gracefully. As of iOS 26 / WWDC25, Look Around 3D imagery is also available on the web via MapKit JS, which matters if we ever build a companion web map.

CLUSTERING AT SCALE = UIKit OR A LIBRARY. Native MKMapView clustering (set annotation.clusteringIdentifier, dequeue MKMarkerAnnotationView, receive MKClusterAnnotation) is mature but adds annotations on the main thread, which stutters with thousands of breweries. For a nationwide catalog, use an off-main-thread QuadTree clusterer (e.g. the ClusterMap library, which works with SwiftUI Map by pre-clustering the data array) or wrap MKMapView. Plan to render clusters, not raw pins, above ~a few hundred annotations in view.

HEATMAP (CHECK-IN DENSITY) HAS TWO IMPLEMENTATIONS. (1) True heatmap: custom MKOverlay + MKOverlayRenderer subclass that paints a radial-gradient density field from check-in coordinates (UIKit-bridged; JDSwiftHeatMap is a reference impl). (2) SwiftUI-native approximation: bucket check-ins into H3/grid cells server-side and render one MapCircle per cell with fillStyle opacity/color scaled by density — fully declarative, no UIKit, good enough for a 'hot right now' layer and toggled as a .mapStyle overlay layer.

iOS 26 (WWDC25) ADDS GEOTOOLBOX + MODERN GEOCODING. New GeoToolbox.PlaceDescriptor describes a place without a MapKit identifier (common name + address/coordinate + optional per-service identifiers), and MKMapItemRequest resolves a PlaceDescriptor back to an MKMapItem — this is the clean bridge between OUR catalog rows and Apple places. CLGeocoder/CLPlacemark are deprecated in favor of MKGeocodingRequest / MKReverseGeocodingRequest and MKAddressRepresentations. 20+ MapKit APIs came to watchOS, and cycling directions were added.

MATCHING APPLE POIs TO OUR CATALOG. MKMapItem.identifier (iOS 18) is a stable per-place ID; persist it on our brewery rows to re-fetch the exact Apple place later. When we only have our own address/coords (no Apple ID), build a GeoToolbox PlaceDescriptor and let MKMapItemRequest resolve it — it prefers a stored MapKit identifier and falls back to coordinate/address. This is the join key strategy: store MKMapItem.identifier the first time we see an Apple match, dedupe by proximity+name otherwise.

CROSS-PLATFORM VERDICT: BOTH RN OPTIONS LOSE THE THREE THINGS THAT MAKE THIS FEATURE GOOD. expo-maps (AppleMaps) is SwiftUI-Map-based, supports markers/annotations/callouts, POI category include/exclude filtering, and openLookAroundAsync — but is iOS-only, still alpha (frequent breaking changes), has NO clustering and NO custom React marker views (system images/monograms only), so it inherits SwiftUI Map's exact gaps with none of the UIKit escape hatch. react-native-maps with PROVIDER_DEFAULT (Apple/MapKit) supports custom markers and community clustering libs (react-native-clusterer/supercluster) but has known New-Architecture/Fabric bugs specifically on Apple Maps custom markers (off-anchor drift on zoom, ignored transforms — confirmed on iOS 18/26-era builds), does NOT expose MKLocalPointsOfInterestRequest POI-category search, the iOS 18 Place Card, or Look Around, and gives no path to a custom MKOverlayRenderer heatmap. Neither wrapper reaches the UIKit MKMapView layer our clustering + heatmap need.
recommendations: BUILD NATIVE SWIFT / SwiftUI. This map is the product's signature surface and depends on exactly the APIs the cross-platform wrappers omit: MKLocalPointsOfInterestRequest category search, iOS 18 Place Card (mapItemDetailSelectionAccessory), Look Around, real clustering, and a custom MKOverlayRenderer heatmap. Ship it as native Swift.

USE SwiftUI Map AS THE DEFAULT, WITH A TARGETED UIViewRepresentable(MKMapView) ESCAPE HATCH for the two things SwiftUI can't do: (a) high-density clustering, (b) the true check-in heatmap renderer. Keep 90% of the screen in idiomatic SwiftUI (Marker/Annotation/selection/camera) and localize UIKit to those two subsystems.

DATA MODEL: treat Apple POIs and our catalog as two sources merged into one view model. Query Apple via MKLocalPointsOfInterestRequest(center:radius:) filtered to [.brewery,.bar,.nightlife,.winery,.distillery] on camera-idle; left-join our Supabase catalog on stored MKMapItem.identifier (fallback: GeoToolbox PlaceDescriptor + proximity/name dedupe). Our rows own taplist, check-in density, and 'verified partner' flags; Apple owns geometry, hours, photos, Look Around.

CALLOUT STRATEGY: on selection, present the native Place Card (.callout on iPad/large, .sheet on iPhone) for hours/photos/directions, and inject a custom section rendering OUR data — current taps, IBU/style chips, live check-in count, and a Look Around thumbnail gated on a non-nil MKLookAroundScene. This is the 'combine Apple POI + our data' win.

PERFORMANCE PLAN: debounce region queries on onMapCameraChange, cap MKLocalSearch calls (Apple rate-limits), cluster above a few hundred annotations, and pre-aggregate the heatmap into grid/H3 cells server-side so the client either draws MapCircle-per-cell (SwiftUI) or feeds a lightweight gradient overlay (UIKit) instead of thousands of raw points.

IF A REACT-NATIVE CODEBASE IS TRULY MANDATED, scope the map as a native module / Expo config-plugin wrapping our own MKMapView SwiftUI view rather than using expo-maps or react-native-maps directly — i.e. you end up writing the native map anyway. Given that, and that the map is core, do NOT go cross-platform for this surface.

ADOPT iOS 26 GEOTOOLBOX NOW for the catalog<->Apple join (PlaceDescriptor + MKMapItemRequest) and migrate off CLGeocoder to MKGeocodingRequest/MKReverseGeocodingRequest to avoid shipping on deprecated geocoding APIs.

SET MINIMUM iOS 18 (ideally 26) for this feature so mapItemDetailSelectionAccessory, MKMapItem.identifier, and the newest POI/Look Around behavior are available without fallbacks; gate older-OS users to a simplified pin-only map if a lower floor is required.
risks: SwiftUI Map has no native clustering or custom cluster views and no MKOverlayRenderer — the heatmap and dense-pin cases FORCE a UIKit MKMapView bridge; budget engineering time for a UIViewRepresentable and its coordinator/delegate glue.

Apple's MKLocalSearch / points-of-interest requests are rate-limited and undocumented in exact thresholds; aggressive per-frame querying on pan/zoom will get throttled — must debounce, cache by region tile, and degrade to catalog-only pins when throttled.
Look Around coverage is city-limited; MKLookAroundScene is frequently nil for smaller-town breweries — the UI must not promise Look Around everywhere and must hide the thumbnail when unavailable.

Apple POI data for craft breweries is uneven (new/small taprooms may be missing or miscategorized as .restaurant/.bar), so relying solely on Apple's brewery layer will undercount — our own catalog must be the source of truth and Apple the enrichment layer, not vice versa.

react-native-maps has active, confirmed Apple-Maps-on-New-Architecture bugs (custom-view marker off-anchor drift on zoom-out, ignored inner transforms) on iOS 18/26-era builds; expo-maps AppleMaps is explicitly alpha with frequent breaking changes and is iOS-only — either RN path is a moving target for a flagship surface.

MKMapItem.identifier stability is 'stable' but not contractually permanent across Apple data refreshes; store it opportunistically but always keep coordinate+name+PlaceDescriptor fallbacks so the catalog join survives an identifier change.

MapKit usage/entitlements: heavy server-side or JS use falls under MapKit JS quotas and Apple's Maps terms (no scraping/rehosting Apple map data); the on-device MKLocalSearch path avoids most of this, but a web companion using MapKit JS would need quota planning.

Deprecations to avoid shipping on: CLGeocoder/CLPlacemark (use MKGeocodingRequest family), and the pre-iOS-17 MapAnnotation/MapMarker/MapUserTrackingButton — code against the iOS 17+ Marker/Annotation and iOS 18+ selection-accessory APIs.
data.details:
ARCHITECTURE RECOMMENDATION (native Swift, SwiftUI-first with surgical UIKit):

VIEW LAYER
- Base: SwiftUI `Map(position: $cameraPosition, selection: $selectedItem)` with `.mapStyle(.standard(pointsOfInterest: .including([.brewery,.bar,.nightlife,.winery,.distillery])))` so even Apple's own basemap POIs are constrained to beer venues.
- Our breweries: `ForEach` of merged view-model items rendered as `Annotation` (custom SwiftUI pin: mug glyph, color by open/closed, small badge = live check-in count) or `Marker(systemImage:"mug.fill", ...)` for the simple tier.
- Selection: bind `selection` to an `MKMapItem`; attach `.mapItemDetailSelectionAccessory(.callout)` (iOS 18) for Apple's Place Card, and present our own taplist/heat section in the accompanying sheet.
- Camera: `.onMapCameraChange(frequency: .onEnd) { ctx in reload(region: ctx.region) }` (debounced) drives search + clustering + heatmap refresh.

SEARCH LAYER
- Nearby: `MKLocalPointsOfInterestRequest(center: userCoord, radius: min(regionRadius, 25_000))`, set `pointOfInterestFilter = MKPointOfInterestFilter(including:[.brewery,.bar,.nightlife,.winery,.distillery])`, run via `MKLocalSearch(request:).start()` -> `[MKMapItem]`.
- Text/search bar: `MKLocalSearch.Request` with `naturalLanguageQuery` + `region` + same filter; or `MKLocalSearchCompleter` for autocomplete.

MERGE / JOIN LAYER
- Key: persist `MKMapItem.identifier` (iOS 18) on our catalog rows. When we only have address/coords, build `GeoToolbox.PlaceDescriptor(...)` and resolve with `MKMapItemRequest` (iOS 26) — it prefers a stored MapKit identifier, falls back to coordinate/address.
- Dedupe unmatched: proximity (<~30 m) + normalized name.
- Ownership split: Apple = geometry, hours, photos, Look Around, Place Card; Us = taplist, styles/IBU, check-in density, partner/verified status, offers.

CLUSTERING (scale)
- Default to SwiftUI Map + a QuadTree pre-clusterer (ClusterMap-style) that clusters the data array off-main-thread and hands SwiftUI a reduced annotation set; render cluster bubbles as `Annotation`s with a count.
- If we need Apple's native cluster animations, use the UIKit escape hatch: `UIViewRepresentable` over `MKMapView`, set `annotation.clusteringIdentifier`, dequeue `MKMarkerAnnotationView`, handle `MKClusterAnnotation` in the delegate.

HEATMAP (check-in density)
- Server pre-aggregates check-ins into grid/H3 cells with a weight.
- SwiftUI path: one `MapCircle(center:radius:)` per hot cell, `.foregroundStyle(...opacity(weight))` — declarative, no UIKit, toggled as a 'Hot now' layer.
- True-gradient path (if desired): UIKit `MKOverlay` + custom `MKOverlayRenderer` painting radial gradients (JDSwiftHeatMap as reference), added to the bridged `MKMapView`.

LOOK AROUND
- `let scene = try? await MKLookAroundSceneRequest(mapItem: item).scene`; if non-nil show `LookAroundPreview(initialScene: scene)` thumbnail in the callout and `.lookAroundViewer(isPresented:$show, scene:$scene)` on tap. Always nil-gate.

iOS 26 / WWDC25 DELTAS TO ADOPT
- GeoToolbox `PlaceDescriptor` + `MKMapItemRequest` for the catalog join.
- Modern geocoding: `MKGeocodingRequest` / `MKReverseGeocodingRequest`, `MKAddressRepresentations` (`fullAddress()`, `cityWithContext()`); stop using CLGeocoder/CLPlacemark.
- Cycling directions (`transportType = .cycling`) for 'route to this brewery'; 20+ MapKit APIs now on watchOS (a companion 'nearest taproom' watch complication is feasible).

CROSS-PLATFORM ANALYSIS
- expo-maps `AppleMaps`: SwiftUI-Map-based; supports markers, annotations, callouts, POI category include/exclude, camera, user location, and `openLookAroundAsync`; BUT iOS-only, alpha (frequent breaking changes), NO clustering, NO custom React marker views (system image/monogram only). Inherits SwiftUI Map's gaps with no UIKit escape hatch.
- react-native-maps `PROVIDER_DEFAULT` (MapKit): custom markers + community clustering (react-native-clusterer/supercluster) work, but confirmed New-Arch/Fabric bugs on Apple custom markers (off-anchor drift, ignored transforms) on iOS 18/26-era builds; does NOT surface MKLocalPointsOfInterestRequest category search, the iOS 18 Place Card, or Look Around; no route to a custom MKOverlayRenderer heatmap.
- Neither reaches the UIKit MKMapView layer our clustering + heatmap require, and neither exposes the iOS 18/26 place APIs that make our callouts and catalog-join first-class.

STRENGTH OF PULL TOWARD NATIVE: STRONG. Three of this feature's differentiators — dense clustering, a real check-in heatmap, and Apple's rich Place Card + Look Around wired to our own tap data — are precisely what the RN/Expo wrappers cannot do today. If cross-platform is mandated we would end up writing a native module wrapping our own MKMapView regardless, so the pragmatic choice for a flagship 'breweries near you' map is native Swift/SwiftUI targeting iOS 18 minimum (iOS 26 preferred).

key_findings: APPLE — 17+ NO LONGER EXISTS. Apple overhauled age ratings in 2025 (live with iOS 26); the bands are now 4+, 9+, 13+, 16+, 18+. An alcohol app must self-rate 18+ (the band for 'frequent references to alcohol/tobacco/drug use'), and you were required to answer the new age-rating questionnaire by Jan 31, 2026, or App Store Connect blocks your updates. Separately, the app must not 'encourage excessive consumption of alcohol' and must not enable actual on-device alcohol purchase in a way that violates review rules; age-gate at first launch (self-attested DOB gate is the norm, but do NOT collect the DOB into your marketing/sale dataset).

APPLE ATT — Selling/sharing data to data brokers IS 'tracking' under Apple's own definition, full stop. Apple defines tracking to include 'sharing user or device data with data brokers' and 'sharing device location data or email lists with a data broker.' So if your business model is selling location+preference data, you MUST show the App Tracking Transparency (ATT) prompt with an NSUserTrackingUsageDescription purpose string, and if the user declines, IDFA returns all-zeros and you may not track. You may NOT gate app functionality on ATT consent or bribe users to consent (Guideline 5.1.2(i)). Carve-outs (no ATT needed) are narrow: on-device-only linking, and sharing to a data broker solely for fraud/security or to a consumer-reporting agency for creditworthiness — data monetization does NOT qualify.

APPLE PRIVACY LABELS + LOCATION STRINGS — You must file accurate Privacy Nutrition Labels in App Store Connect declaring 'Location' and 'Preference/Purchase History' under BOTH 'Data Linked to You' and, critically, 'Data Used to Track You' (that third label is what triggers if you sell/share). A Privacy Manifest (PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy) is also required declaring data types, tracking domains, and any 'required-reason' APIs. For location, request the least privilege: use 'When In Use' (NSLocationWhenInUseUsageDescription) — only request 'Always' (NSLocationAlwaysAndWhenInUseUsageDescription) if you have a genuine background feature, because 'Always' + data sale is a red flag for both Apple review and the FTC. Permission strings must state the real purpose in plain language.

FTC IS THE BIGGEST LANDMINE FOR THIS EXACT BUSINESS. In 2024 the FTC brought/settled a wave of location-data-broker cases — X-Mode/Outlogic, InMarket, Gravy Analytics/Venntel, Mobilewalla, and the Kochava matter (Kochava was barred in 2026 from selling sensitive location data). The through-line: you may NOT sell, license, or disclose precise location tied to 'sensitive locations' without AFFIRMATIVE EXPRESS CONSENT that is tied to a specific service the consumer requested. The FTC's sensitive-location list explicitly includes substance-abuse/addiction-treatment facilities — directly relevant to an ALCOHOL app whose users' movements can reveal recovery centers, AA meetings, medical, and religious sites. Selling raw bidstream/precise location without verifying informed consent is treated as an unfair practice regardless of CCPA compliance.

CCPA/CPRA — SELLING + SHARING BOTH NEED OPT-OUT, AND LOCATION IS 'SENSITIVE.' Precise geolocation is Sensitive Personal Information (SPI) under CPRA, triggering the 'Limit the Use of My Sensitive Personal Information' right in addition to 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Info.' Both links (or one combined link, or a compliant opt-out preference mechanism) must appear on your homepage/app and in the privacy policy. You must honor the Global Privacy Control (GPC) opt-out preference signal automatically. New CCPA regs effective Jan 1, 2026 require a documented RISK ASSESSMENT before you sell/share PI or process SPI, and a cybersecurity audit if you derive ≥50% of revenue from selling/sharing data (very likely for a data-monetization business). ADMT rules phase in Jan 1, 2027.

CALIFORNIA DELETE ACT / DROP — YOU WILL LIKELY BE A REGISTERED DATA BROKER. If you sell personal info of consumers 'with whom you do not have a direct relationship' (i.e., you sell your users' data to third parties who never dealt with those users), you meet California's data-broker definition and must register with the CPPA every year between Jan 1–31 (fee ~$6,600), or face $200/day fines. The CPPA's DROP platform went live Jan 1, 2026 and registered brokers must begin honoring bulk deletion requests starting Aug 1, 2026 (every 45 days). The CPPA launched a data-broker 'strike force' and is actively fining non-registrants in 2026.

GDPR — DATA SALE/LOCATION NEEDS CONSENT, NOT LEGITIMATE INTEREST. For EU/UK users, selling personal data to third parties and processing smartphone location for monetization cannot rest on 'legitimate interest' — you need freely-given, specific, informed, unambiguous OPT-IN consent (Art. 6(1)(a)), separate from your terms, granular per purpose, as easy to withdraw as to give, with no 'tracking wall' (can't make app access conditional on consent to sale). ePrivacy requires prior consent before accessing/storing on the device (SDKs, identifiers). GDPR anonymization is a HIGHER bar than CCPA de-identification: it must be irreversible; pseudonymized or coarsely-aggregated location often still counts as personal data and re-identifies easily.

M&A / COMPANY-SALE PATH IS ACTUALLY THE EASIER ONE — IF SET UP RIGHT. Under CCPA/CPRA, transferring personal info in a merger, acquisition, or bankruptcy where the buyer assumes control is EXEMPT from the 'sale' definition — but only if (a) your privacy policy/notice-at-collection disclosed this at the time of collection, (b) the buyer honors the original privacy promises and can't make material retroactive changes without fresh notice + opt-out, and (c) per AB 1824 (effective Jan 1, 2025) the acquirer MUST carry over and honor every 'do not sell/share' opt-out the seller received. Under GDPR the M&A transfer still needs a lawful basis and clear disclosure to data subjects, but a straight change-of-control that preserves purposes is generally workable. The takeaway: an eventual COMPANY sale is clean if you disclose it up front; an ongoing DATA sale is the far higher-risk activity.
recommendations: DAY-ONE CHECKLIST — CONSENT ARCHITECTURE: Build a layered, per-purpose consent capture from launch: (1) neutral pre-permission explainer -> iOS location prompt requesting 'When In Use' only; (2) a SEPARATE, unbundled, opt-in toggle (default OFF) for 'Share/sell my data with partners' and another for 'Use my precise location for [named purpose]'; (3) the ATT system prompt with an honest purpose string. Never bundle these; never pre-check; make withdrawal one tap. Log every consent event with timestamp, exact text shown, version, and scope in an immutable consent-ledger — this record is what makes a future data sale or acquisition defensible.

DAY-ONE CHECKLIST — APPLE: Set age rating to 18+ and complete the 2025 age-rating questionnaire; add an age-gate at first launch. File Privacy Nutrition Labels declaring Location + Preferences/Purchases under 'Data Linked to You' AND 'Data Used to Track You.' Ship a PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy manifest listing data types, tracking domains, and required-reason APIs; audit every third-party SDK (ad/analytics/location) because you are liable for their tracking. Write plain-purpose NSLocationWhenInUseUsageDescription / NSUserTrackingUsageDescription strings. Do not gate features on ATT or incentivize consent.

DAY-ONE CHECKLIST — U.S. STATE LAW: Publish a 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' link AND a 'Limit the Use of My Sensitive Personal Information' link (or a single combined opt-out) on the app/site homepage and in the privacy policy. Implement automatic Global Privacy Control (GPC) honoring. Classify precise geolocation as Sensitive PI. Assume you are a California data broker: calendar the Jan 1–31 CPPA registration and budget the ~$6,600 fee; build DROP bulk-deletion handling (live Aug 1, 2026, 45-day cycle). Register in other data-broker-registry states too (Texas, Oregon, Vermont).

DAY-ONE CHECKLIST — FTC-PROOFING (highest priority for this model): Treat precise location as toxic unless you have affirmative express consent tied to a specific requested feature. Maintain and enforce a 'sensitive location' exclusion/suppression list (addiction-treatment/AA sites, medical, reproductive, religious, shelters, union halls) so those geopoints are never in what you sell — this is the exact FTC hot button for an alcohol app. Contractually bind every data buyer to no-reidentification and no-sensitive-use terms, and audit them. Do not sell raw RTB/bidstream location. Minimize precision (e.g., truncate/round) and retention.

STRUCTURE THE CONSENT SO A FUTURE SALE IS CLEAN: In the notice-at-collection and privacy policy from day one, expressly state (a) that data (including location and preferences) may be SOLD/SHARED with third parties for the stated commercial purposes, and (b) that personal information may be TRANSFERRED as an asset in a merger, acquisition, financing, bankruptcy, or change-of-control. This dual disclosure is what lets an eventual company sale fall inside the CCPA business-transfer exemption and gives GDPR transparency cover. Ensure your consent records and opt-out list are portable so a buyer can inherit and honor AB 1824 opt-outs.

SEGREGATE EU/UK: Consent (opt-in, unbundled, withdrawable) is mandatory for selling data and for location monetization there — legitimate interest will not hold. Gate ad/location SDKs behind a compliant CMP (IAB TCF v2.3 by the Feb 28, 2026 deadline if you use the framework), fire no non-essential SDK before consent, and appoint an EU/UK representative and (given large-scale location tracking) likely a DPO plus a DPIA. Keep the EU dataset architecturally separable so you can exclude it from a data sale if a buyer/partner isn't GDPR-comfortable.

ANONYMIZATION / AGGREGATION STANDARD TO ADOPT: For anything you sell as 'aggregate/de-identified,' meet the strict tier: apply k-anonymity (k>=some threshold, commonly k>=5 or higher for location, with l-diversity/t-closeness), coarsen geohash precision, add differential-privacy noise for statistical products, and publicly commit + contractually prohibit re-identification (a CCPA de-identification requirement). Remember GDPR anonymization must be IRREVERSIBLE to leave scope — coarse/pseudonymized location usually does not qualify, so still treat it as personal data for EU users. Keep a documented re-identification-risk assessment on file.

GOVERNANCE FROM DAY ONE: Draft a data-monetization risk assessment (now a Jan 1, 2026 CCPA requirement before selling/sharing or processing SPI) and refresh it; plan the cybersecurity audit if data-sale revenue >=50%. Data-map every field, its source consent, and its legal basis. Put Data Processing / Data Sharing Agreements (with CCPA 'service provider vs. third party' language and GDPR SCCs where relevant) in place with every partner and buyer. Give the acquirer a clean diligence package: consent ledger, opt-out list, DPAs, broker registrations, and no sensitive-location leakage — that is what preserves deal value and avoids successor liability.
risks: FTC UNFAIRNESS EXPOSURE IS THE #1 RISK: an alcohol app selling precise location is squarely in the FTC's 2024–2026 enforcement lane (X-Mode/Outlogic, InMarket, Gravy/Venntel, Mobilewalla, Kochava). Selling location that can reveal AA/addiction-recovery/medical/religious visits without affirmative express, service-tied consent can produce a consent decree, a 20-year compliance regime, sensitive-data deletion, and an outright ban on the core business model — CCPA compliance does NOT immunize you from this.

APP REJECTION / REMOVAL: mis-declared Privacy Nutrition Labels, a missing/incorrect Privacy Manifest, failing to show ATT while selling to brokers, gating features on ATT, or a wrong age rating (must be 18+, and the Jan 31 2026 questionnaire was mandatory) can get the app rejected or pulled. A rogue third-party SDK that tracks without ATT is YOUR violation.

DATA-BROKER NON-REGISTRATION: failing to register under California's Delete Act ($200/day + strike-force enforcement now active in 2026) and equivalent Texas/Oregon/Vermont registries; and failing to wire up DROP bulk deletions (brokers must comply from Aug 1, 2026).

BROKEN M&A / DATA-SALE CONSENT CHAIN: if the original notice-at-collection did not disclose sale AND asset-transfer, a later data sale is an unconsented 'sale' and a company sale may fall outside the CCPA business-transfer exemption; under AB 1824 an acquirer inherits (and can be liable for ignoring) prior opt-outs, and can't make material retroactive privacy-policy changes — this can blow up or discount a deal in diligence (successor liability).

GDPR/ePrivacy INVALID CONSENT: relying on legitimate interest for data sale/location, bundled or pre-ticked consent, tracking walls, or firing SDKs before consent = invalid consent, exposing up to 4% of global turnover; 'anonymized' location that re-identifies is still personal data and its sale is an unlawful transfer.

RE-IDENTIFICATION / SENSITIVE-INFERENCE: aggregated or 'de-identified' location frequently re-identifies (home + work uniquely fingerprint a person) and can infer health/religion — creating liability even for data you believed was safe to sell, and voiding de-identification/anonymization claims.
data.details:
CONTEXT SPECIFIC TO THIS APP: A consumer alcohol app collecting location + preference data with the explicit intent to (a) sell/share data and (b) be acquired sits at the intersection of the three hardest privacy regimes right now: Apple's tracking rules, the FTC's location-data-broker crackdown, and California's data-broker/Delete-Act regime. The single most important reframe: the FTC — not the App Store or CCPA — is the existential risk, because the FTC has repeatedly used its unfairness authority to BAN companies from selling sensitive location data outright, and an alcohol app's location trails can expose addiction-treatment, medical, and religious visits (all on the FTC's sensitive-location list).

WHY 17+ IS A TRAP IN THE PROMPT: Apple retired the old 4+/9+/12+/17+ scheme in 2025. The current bands are 4+, 9+, 13+, 16+, 18+. An alcohol-themed app is 18+ ('frequent references to alcohol/tobacco/drug use'). Every developer had to re-answer the new age-rating questionnaire by Jan 31, 2026 or lose the ability to ship updates. So the correct answer to 'age-gate 17+' is: age-gate to 18+ under the new system, plus a launch DOB gate — and keep that DOB out of your saleable dataset.

WHY ATT APPLIES: Apple's own definition of 'tracking' explicitly includes 'sharing user or device data with data brokers' and 'sharing device location data ... with a data broker.' Because the whole point of this business is to sell location+preference data, ATT is mandatory, the IDFA is zeroed on decline, and you cannot gate the app or bribe consent (5.1.2(i)). The only ATT carve-outs (on-device-only, fraud/security, credit-reporting) do not cover monetization.

DATA SALE vs COMPANY SALE — THE KEY LEGAL DISTINCTION: These are governed differently. An ONGOING DATA SALE is a regulated 'sale/share' under CCPA (opt-out + GPC + SPI limit + risk assessment + likely data-broker registration) and requires opt-in consent under GDPR — this is the high-friction, high-risk activity. A ONE-TIME COMPANY SALE (M&A) is EXEMPT from the CCPA 'sale' definition via the business-transfer exception, provided you disclosed asset-transfer up front, the buyer honors original promises (no material retroactive change), and — under AB 1824 (Jan 1, 2025) — the buyer carries over every prior do-not-sell/share opt-out. Practical implication: draft your notice-at-collection to disclose BOTH sale and asset-transfer on day one; that dual disclosure is what keeps both paths clean and preserves acquisition value in diligence.

CONSENT ENGINEERING FOR A CLEAN EXIT: The asset that makes a future sale (of data or company) legally clean is a defensible consent ledger: per-purpose, unbundled, opt-in (for sale/location/EU), default-off, timestamped, versioned, with the exact UI text stored, plus a portable opt-out/GPC suppression list the buyer can inherit. Bundled or pre-checked consent is worthless and can retroactively taint the entire dataset's saleability.

ANONYMIZATION REALITY CHECK: CCPA 'de-identification' (reasonably not identifiable + public commitment + contractual no-reID) is a LOWER bar than GDPR 'anonymization' (irreversible). Location is notoriously re-identifiable (home+work fingerprints an individual), so 'aggregated location' you plan to sell may still be personal data — adopt k-anonymity with meaningful k, geohash coarsening, differential privacy for statistical outputs, contractual no-reID, and a documented re-ID risk assessment; still treat EU location as personal data.

NET POSTURE: The company sale is achievable with day-one disclosure. The ongoing data sale is where the business could be shut down — treat precise/sensitive location as the crown-jewel liability, gate it behind affirmative express service-tied consent, suppress all sensitive-location geopoints before any sale, and never sell raw bidstream location.
